# Chapter 4. Surplus Value and Profit

```{=html}
<p class="fst">The same difference that exists between <em>value</em> and <em>price</em> also prevails between surplus value and profit. What interests the practical man, the seller and buyer of commodities, is their price. Accordingly, he is also interested only in the laws of price, because knowledge of them can be useful to him in his commercial calculations and speculations. The laws of value, however, which underlie price, interest only the theoretician, whose concern is not to buy as cheaply and sell as dearly as possible, but to investigate the <em>social interconnections</em> that are established through commodity production.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Thus the practical capitalist too is not interested in surplus value, but in profit. He does not want to investigate the relationship between capital and labour, but to make as large a profit as possible. But with what expenditure of labour this profit is created is, at first, a matter of great indifference to him. For it is not <em>his</em> labour that creates it. It is, however, his <em>money</em> with which it is created. He therefore places the surplus value obtained not in relation to the quantity of labour that had to be expended in its production, but to the quantity of money that he had to advance for the purpose. If the movement of the creation of surplus value is represented by the formula <em>M—C—(M + m)</em>, then the capitalist measures his profit by the ratio of <em>m</em> to <em>M</em>. But this ratio is by no means the same as that between <em>v</em> and <em>m</em>, between variable capital and surplus value. The sum of money that the capitalist had to advance for production must suffice not merely to pay wages, but also for factory buildings, machines, raw materials, auxiliary materials—in short, all that Marx summarises under the term “constant capital.” By this alone it comes about that even where surplus value and profit fully coincide, the rate of profit is nevertheless a different one from the rate of surplus value. If the rate of surplus value is expressed by the formula <em>v</em> : <em>m</em>, then the rate of profit is expressed by the formula <em>(c + v)</em> : <em>m</em>.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It should further be noted that for many branches of production, notably agriculture, the year forms a natural period of production, at the close of which production begins anew. Hence the custom has developed of calculating the rate of profit by the ratio of the quantity of profit obtained in <em>one year</em> to the quantity of capital advanced for production in <em>that same year</em>.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is clear from the outset that the rate of profit must differ from the rate of surplus value.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In the previous chapter we had chosen the example of a capital of 5000 marks; of this, 4100 marks formed the constant, 900 marks the variable capital; 900 marks the surplus value. The rate of surplus value was therefore 900 : 900 = 100 per cent. The rate of profit, by contrast, is in this case 5000 : 900 = 18 per cent.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But between the rate of surplus value and that of profit another difference soon emerges besides this purely formal one of a different kind of calculation.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is clear that the same rate of surplus value must yield different rates of profit if the composition of the capital is a different one, if to the same quantity of wages there correspond different quantities of constant capital. But depending on the technical character and the degree of technical development, this composition is necessarily different in every branch of production.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="quoteb">“The value-composition of capital, in so far as it is determined by, and reflects, its technical composition, we call the <em>organic composition</em> of capital ... We therefore call capitals which contain, in percentage terms, more constant, and thus less variable, capital than the social average capital: capitals of <em>higher composition</em>. Conversely, those in which the constant capital occupies a relatively smaller and the variable a larger space than in the social average capital, we call capitals of <em>lower composition</em>. Finally, we call capitals of <em>average composition</em> those whose composition coincides with that of the social average capital” (<strong>Capital</strong>, 3, vol. 1, pp. 124, 142).</p>
```

```{=html}
<p class="fst">Let us now see how the rate of profit takes shape under the influence of the different compositions. Let us take three enterprises from three different branches of production. The one is still technically backward, employs few machines in relation to the number of workers, needs no large factory buildings &amp;c. Let it have a low organic composition. The second an average one; the third, by contrast, let it be so highly developed that a large sum of value in machines and buildings falls upon each worker. Let the organic composition of its capital be a high one.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>We make the example as simple as possible, and therefore assume that in all three branches of production the rate of surplus value is the same, and that the whole advanced capital turns over once a year, that is, that it is entirely consumed in production in one year and that the product is sold only at the end of the year, but then in its entirety. These are assumptions that scarcely ever occur in reality, but which we must make if the example is not to become too complicated and confusing.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In each of the three enterprises 100 workers are employed at an annual wage of 1000 marks each. Let the rate of surplus value everywhere amount to 100 per cent; the mass of wages therefore 100,000 marks, and likewise the mass of surplus value 100,000 marks. But let the constant capital amount in enterprise <em>A</em> to 100,000 marks, in enterprise <em>B</em> to 300,000 marks, and finally in the third enterprise <em>C</em> to 500,000 marks.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Then we have</p>
```

```{=html}
<table align="center" cellpadding="3" cellspacing="3"> <tr valign="top"> <td> <p class="smc">Enterprise</p> </td> <td colspan="3"> <p class="smc">Capital</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Surplus value</p> </td> <td rowspan="2"> <p class="smc">Rate of surplus value,<br/> that is<br/> the ratio of<br/> surplus value to<br/> constant capital</p> </td> <td rowspan="2"> <p class="smc">Rate of profit,<br/> that is<br/> the ratio of<br/> surplus value to<br/> total capital</p> </td> </tr> <tr valign="bottom"> <td> <p class="smc">  </p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">variable</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">constant</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Total</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc"> </p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc"> </p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Marks</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Marks</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Marks</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Marks</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Per cent</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Per cent</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc"><em>A</em></p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">   200.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">50   </p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc"><em>B</em></p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">300.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">   400.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">25   </p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc"><em>C</em></p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">500.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">   600.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">16,6</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc">Together</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">300.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">900.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">1.200.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">300.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">25   </p> </td> </tr> </table>
```

```{=html}
<p class="fst">With an equal rate of surplus value, the rates of profit will thus be very different if the commodities are sold exactly at their values.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This difference in the rates of profit is, however, a condition that cannot persist under the capitalist mode of production. The capitalist produces solely for the sake of profit, not in order to satisfy any need. What he produces is a matter of indifference to him, whether sewing-needles or locomotives, boot-blacking or Cologne water; the main thing is that he pockets as high a profit as possible for his money. What, then, will be the consequence if in one branch the enterprises yield 50 per cent and in another only 17 per cent? Capital will avoid the latter as much as it can and turn with all its might to the former. <em>A</em> will encounter strong competition, the production of the commodities of this branch will grow rapidly, while in <em>C</em> it will decline.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Here we come to the field of competition, that of demand and supply. We have already seen that value and price are two different things, even though the latter is determined by the former. Among the causes that bring it about that prices deviate from values, standing now higher, now lower, the most important is the fluctuation in the demand of those willing to buy and in the supply of the sellers.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Under free competition, demand and supply are the regulators of the present-day mode of production, which would otherwise fall into the worst anarchy, since it is not planfully regulated but is carried on by private enterprises, each of which produces on its own according to the discretion of its owner or manager. Demand and supply see to it that the available labour-powers are allotted among the various branches of production in such a way that, in general, each produces as much as society, under the given conditions, demands. Of course this holds only in general, not in particular, not in every single case. Rather, given the planlessness of the present-day mode of production, either too much or too little of one commodity or another is always produced, and only afterwards is it brought about, through the play of demand and supply, through the fall or rise of prices, that production is restricted or expanded in accordance with social need.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>If more of a commodity is produced than the purchasing members of society can or will buy of it at a certain level of price, which in the last instance is determined by its value, then it falls in price, whereby the circle of those members of society who can or will buy it is widened. But with the price the profit falls; if it falls below the average, then capital is thereby deterred from the branch of production in question, its production is reduced, and thereby the price is again raised until it reaches the level corresponding to the average profit.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Conversely: if the price rises above this high point, because less of the commodity is produced than corresponds to the demand of the buyers, then the profit rises with it too. Capital is attracted to the branch of production, flows into it, expands production, whereupon prices again sink to the level yielding the average profit. Around this level prices move without interruption, now rising above it, now sinking below it, and only through this wave-like movement is the level established, which is always present only as a tendency, a striving, not as a permanent condition.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This action of demand and supply must also work against those inequalities in the rates of profit which arise from the inequality in the organic composition of capital.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In branch <em>C</em> production will decrease and prices, and with them profits, will rise. In branch <em>A</em> production will grow and prices will fall. The one as well as the other will continue until the profits have equalised and have reached the level of the average of the totality of the rates of profit. We have assumed that <em>B</em> represents the average organic composition of capital, and hence that its rate of profit represents the average rate of profit. Then the profit in the three enterprises will take the following shape:</p>
```

```{=html}
<table align="center" cellpadding="3" cellspacing="3"> <tr> <td> <p class="smc">Enterprise</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Total capital</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Surplus value</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Rate of surplus value</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Rate of profit</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Profit</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc"> </p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Marks</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Marks</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Per cent</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Per cent</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Marks</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc"><em>A</em></p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">   200.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">25</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  50.000</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc"><em>B</em></p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">   400.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">25</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100.000</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc"><em>C</em></p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">   600.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">25</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">150.000</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc">Together</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">1.200.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">300.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">25</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">300.000</p> </td> </tr> </table>
```

```{=html}
<p class="fst">This equalisation of the rate of profit was, however, only made possible by the prices of the commodities departing from the values of the commodities. Since, according to our assumption, the total advanced capital turns over once in a year and appears in the value of the annual product, we now find the following relationship between the value and the price of the annual product of each enterprise:</p>
```

```{=html}
<table align="center" cellpadding="3" cellspacing="3"> <tr valign="top"> <td> <p class="smc">Enterprise</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Total capital</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Surplus value</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Value of the<br/> total product<br/> (costs of production<br/> + surplus value)</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Profit</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Price of<br/> production of the<br/> total product<br/> (costs of production<br/> + profit)</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc"> </p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Marks</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Marks</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Marks</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Marks</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">Marks</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc"><em>A</em></p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">   200.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">   300.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">  50.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">   250.000</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc"><em>B</em></p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">   400.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">   500.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">   500.000</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc"><em>C</em></p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">   600.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">100.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">   700.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">150.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">   750.000</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="smc">Together</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">1.200.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">300.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">1.500.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">300.000</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">1.500.000</p> </td> </tr> </table>
```

```{=html}
<p class="fst">If we assume that the annual product of each enterprise divides into 10,000 individual commodities, then we have for the single commodity</p>
```

```{=html}
<table align="center" cellpadding="3" cellspacing="3"> <tr> <td> <p class="smc"> </p> </td> <td rowspan="3"> <p class="smc"> </p> </td> <td> <p class="smc"><em>A</em></p> </td> <td rowspan="3"> <p class="smc"> </p> </td> <td> <p class="smc"><em>B</em></p> </td> <td rowspan="3"> <p class="smc"> </p> </td> <td> <p class="smc"><em>C</em></p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="sm1">Value</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">30 Marks</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">50 Marks</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">70 Marks</p> </td> </tr> <tr> <td> <p class="sm1">Price of production</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">25 Marks</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">50 Marks</p> </td> <td> <p class="smc">75 Marks</p> </td> </tr> </table>
```

```{=html}
<p class="fst">In reality the process does not take place in such a way that each capitalist first obtains the full surplus value, so that the capitalists of the one branch make 50 and those of the other only 17 per cent profit. Differences of this kind occur only in the beginnings of the capitalist mode of production, or still today in regions and branches of industry of which this mode of production has newly taken hold. Under developed capitalist production a customary average rate of profit forms, which the capitalists take as their basis from the outset in their price calculations — which of course does not exclude their using every opportunity to go beyond this price, while they regard it as a loss if they obtain a lower price, and hence also a lower rate of profit. This price, which is formed from the costs of production (the variable and constant capital expended), to which is added the “customary” profit, appears to the capitalist as the “natural” one. Marx calls it the <em>price of production.</em> It consists of the <em>cost price</em> (the amount of the variable and constant capital) and the <em>average profit.</em></p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Under the developed capitalist mode of production it is not the <em>value,</em> but the <em>price of production</em> that forms the level around which the market prices, under the influence of demand and supply, fluctuate back and forth in wave-lines. The price of production itself, however, does not float in the air, but has its foundation in value.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The opponents of the Marxian theory of value like to assert that Marx himself overturned his own theory, which he develops in the first volume of <strong>Capital</strong>, by means of the third volume, in which he demonstrated that, in consequence of the striving after equalisation of profits under developed capitalist commodity production, the prices of most commodities permanently deviate from their values, in that the prices of one half of these commodities stand permanently just as much below their values as those of the other half stand above them. But Marx would only have overturned his theory of value if he had shown that prices are <em>independent</em> of their values. Far from doing so, the third volume of <strong>Capital</strong> proves rather that the prices of production, around which the market prices oscillate, stand in the fullest dependence on the law of value, without which they become inexplicable. Precisely that factor, the average profit, which causes the deviations of the prices of production from the values, can only be explained from the laws of surplus value, which in turn proceed from those of value. If we do not assume that the total mass of surplus value existing in society and the total mass of profit with its offshoots (interest, ground rent, with which we shall not deal further here) are equivalent, then we lose every ground of explanation for why the average rate of profit is a definite magnitude under given conditions.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The law of the value of commodities is not annulled by the fact that, under developed capitalist production, a new intermediate link steps between value and price through the average rate of profit and the price of production dependent on it. If one wanted to infer from this its invalidity, then one would also have to assume the invalidity of the law of falling bodies, because the fall of bodies in water encounters still more resistance than in air.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The Marxian theory of the production price cannot be separated from his theories of value and surplus value. Far from carrying these <em>ad absurdum</em>, it rather constitutes their completion. The theory of the production price furnishes us the key to a series of phenomena upon which the relations of the ruling classes among one another rest — to the antagonism between capital (profit) and landed property (ground rent), between industrial capital (industrial profit) and money capital (interest), etc. It furnishes us still more: the key to a series of value theories, and thereby also to their refutation, for a series of theories of value are at bottom merely theories of the production price, which they regard as the ultimate determining ground of the market prices.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>This may be the right place to cast a glance at those value theories which deny the determination of value by labour. As of those mentioned above, one can say of them all that they are not theories of value at all, that by value they understand something which is not value at all: use value, production price, average price.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Now one says, to be sure: every theoretician has the right to understand by value whatever he wishes. We have only to ask whether his explanation of what he understands by value is correct or not. Whether it is then a theory of use value or of price or whatever else, is no concern of ours.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But in every other science such a conception would not be taken seriously, being quite unscientific naïveté. Take, for example, the atomic theory. What would one say to the conception that every researcher is free to understand by atom whatever he wishes, say a molecule or a cell; that it is all the same, provided he only gives a correct cell theory, whether he calls it atomic theory or not? One would answer him at once that in the case of the atom it is not a matter of a name that one can attach at pleasure now to this, now to that thing, but of quite <em>definite processes,</em> to whose explanation the theory of the atom is to serve, processes which, among other things, also underlie the formation of the molecule or the cell. One can accept or reject the atomic theory, that is, explain the processes in question through it or otherwise; but it would be a gross scientific blunder to call an atom a product of those processes which, according to the theory, are determined by the arrangement of the atoms. One must never confuse the fundamental with what is derived from it.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>On this there is no doubt possible in natural science. The processes of political economy are more complicated; nevertheless the same must hold for them as for natural science. It is quite definite social relations and processes that are to be explained by the law of value, and it will not do to designate and treat as laws of value the laws of other relations and processes conditioned by value.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The process which every value theory wishes to and must explain is that of the exchange of two commodities; the social relation which it wishes to and must explain is that between two commodity owners who exchange their commodities with one another. The process of commodity exchange, out of which sale and purchase then develop, is the fundamental one which keeps the whole economic machinery of present-day society in motion. Every explanation of this machinery must therefore proceed from the investigation of the law that regulates commodity exchange, and that is precisely the law of value. If one wished to understand by the law of value the explanation of some other process, then one would have to give a special name to the law which underlies the exchange of commodities. But none of the value theories does this. Each therefore wishes to explain the same process.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But if one keeps before one's eyes the process which the law of value has to explain, then it becomes easily clear that one must above all strictly distinguish <em>use value</em> and <em>exchange value</em> from one another, and must not allow oneself to be misled by the little word value, which occurs in both designations, into regarding them as equivalent. Many a value theory explains value out of the usefulness of an object. The more useful, the more valuable. That is correct if by more valuable the greater use value is understood, false if the greater exchange value is meant thereby.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Use value, the usefulness of a thing, designates a relation between the individual human being, the consumer, and this thing, but not a social relation, the relation between two human beings, as the exchange relation is. Would one perhaps wish to say that equally useful objects are exchanged against one another in equal quantities? But exchange or sale consists for the most part in the fact that each seller gives away things which have for him no use value, no usefulness.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>When the baker and his people are satisfied, the bread which they have baked and sell has no use value any longer for them. If the baker found no purchaser for it, he would not know what to do with it. On the other hand, this same bread can be of the greatest use value for a worker passing by the baker's who has not yet eaten anything that day. The exchange value of the bread, however, is the same for both parties.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Let us assume that the passing worker is a basket-maker who goes about hawking his baskets. The baker needs a basket; this has great use value for him, whereas for the worker it has none at all. The latter has a mass of baskets standing at home, but nothing to put into them. He gladly gives a basket for a number of loaves of bread. But in what proportion will basket and bread be exchanged if their owners look to usefulness? How many pieces of bread are just as useful for the basket-maker as a basket is for the baker? It is clear that one cannot compare the usefulnesses of two different use values with one another at all; they cannot be brought into a numerical relation with one another. If the basket-maker receives five loaves of bread for his basket, it would be absurd to say that a basket is five times as useful or (in this sense) as valuable as a loaf of bread. The usefulnesses of the different commodities are not measurable against one another.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In the case of different pieces <em>of the same kind of commodity</em> one can, to be sure, ascertain a higher or lesser degree of their use value. A durable pair of boots has a greater use value than a less durable one, and I shall gladly pay more for it — if I have the necessary money for it. A bottle of Johannisberger has a greater use value and exchange value than a bottle of Spandauer or Grüneberger. Thus, so it seems, use value is after all an element of the commodity value.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But it only seems so. If the greater use value created the greater commodity value, then the question arises why not every producer produces only the best qualities? Why does not every shoemaker produce only excellent shoes, why does not every vintner grow the best brands? The answer is simple. In the case of shoes the better quality is either a consequence of better raw material, etc., which costs more labour and money, or a consequence of better labour, that is, given average skill of the worker, of greater expenditure of labour. For this reason, and not on account of the higher use value, the solid shoes are dearer. It is well known that one says the dearest commodities are the cheapest, that is, their use value exceeds that of the lower qualities to a far higher degree than their commodity value exceeds that of the latter. A pair of boots at 12 marks lasts perhaps twice as long as one at 10 marks.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But the higher price of particular sorts of wine derives from the fact that they can only be cultivated in certain places. Here the law of value loses its validity altogether, because here we are dealing with a monopoly. The law of value, however, presupposes free competition.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Wherever, within the same kind of commodity, differences of quality condition differences of price, these can always be traced back either to differences in the expenditure of labour or to monopoly conditions.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Still other theories of value confuse value with price. They explain value out of the relation of demand and supply. But these only explain why the prices of a particular commodity always oscillate around its value (or, respectively, its production price); they do not, however, explain why on average the price of one commodity always stands so much higher than that of another, why, for example, for centuries a pound of gold was on average thirteen times as dear as silver.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>If the explanation of value by demand and supply wishes to make these permanent price differences of the various commodities comprehensible, then nothing remains for it but a bashful recourse to the labour theory of value. To the question, whence it comes that the one commodity is permanently so much dearer than the other, it answers that this derives from its greater <em>rarity</em>, which brings it about that its supply is permanently smaller than that of the other. But in order to bring the same quantity of a commodity that is rarer to the market as of one that occurs more frequently, precisely <em>more labour</em> is required. It makes hardly any difference whether I say that a pound of gold was thirteen times as dear as a pound of silver because it was found thirteen times as rarely, or because it cost thirteen times as much labour to produce a pound of gold as a pound of silver. As soon as the theorist does not place himself at the mere merchant's standpoint, on which in the market only the price of commodities interests him, and not the manner in which these were obtained, as soon as he digs deeper and investigates how the commodities that came to market were produced, then he always finds that the values of commodities are determined by the process of <em>production</em>, created in the <em>workshop</em> and not in the <em>market</em>. To the bourgeois theorists, to be sure, the market usually lies closer than the workshop, and that is why they as a rule stand so uncomprehendingly opposed to the labour theory of value.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In the market, value is merely transformed into money, into price; first into represented money, the price demand, and then into real money, when the commodity is sold. The more the capitalist economy develops, the more intermediate links insert themselves between workshop and market, between the producer and the seller to the consumer, the greater can be the deviations, thereby conditioned, of the price actually attained from the theoretically determined value. But this does not prevent that in the last instance it is always the conditions of production that determine the value of commodities, and that the price always remains dependent upon them, however much this dependence may be a mediated one.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The capitalist practitioners themselves determine the value of commodities out of their conditions of production. To be sure, by this they do not understand the labour-time socially necessary for their manufacture, but the costs of production (wages, outlays for machines, raw material, etc.), increased by the average profit. Accordingly, a whole series of theorists also declares that value is determined by the costs of production.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But what is correct from the standpoint of the capitalist practitioner becomes nonsensical from the standpoint of theory, which has not to calculate the respective normal price, but to trace the social processes of the capitalist mode of production back to their ultimate grounds.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>First of all, what are the costs of production? A definite sum of money. They therefore already presuppose money. The determination of value by production thus means that value is to be explained by money, not the reverse. The horse is bridled by the tail.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>The costs of production are a given sum of values – value of labour-power (wages), value of the means of production, value of the profit. Out of this sum of values the value is explained. One sees, this determination of value turns in a circle.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Now let us take, however, a commodity producer, say a peasant weaver, of whom we presuppose that he produces everything himself: he grows his food, likewise the raw material, the flax that his daughters spin, and he makes the loom himself out of his own wood: where, in this man's case, do the costs of production remain? He has no money outlays at all; his product costs him merely labour, nothing but labour.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Now let us go a step further to a higher stage of production, the handicraft weaver. This one already has to make money expenditures; he has costs of production. He must buy the loom, the yarn, also his food. These are his costs of production. But will he calculate the value of the linen he produces according to these? Then his trade will hardly have the much-vaunted golden footing; it will yield him no surplus that he could set aside. And one part of his costs of production – his outlay for food and the loom – remains the same, whether he works 4 or 12 hours in the day. Will he therefore not calculate the product of 12 hours higher than that of 4 hours – apart from the raw material? One sees, he too will reckon his labour, as value-forming, in addition to the costs of the material.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Only for the capitalist does the matter take a different shape. The product costs him no labour at all, but only money. He pays not merely for the means of production, but also for the labour with money; for him, therefore, all the conditions of production resolve themselves into a money outlay, and this appears to him as value-forming. But he would make a strange face were one to assure him that the value of his products is equal to the amount he has expended for their production. He does not have production carried on merely to bring back in his money outlay for the production. He wants also to make a profit. This is the reason why he gives up his money for production at all, instead of consuming it. He therefore adds to the costs of production the "customary" gain. The price so determined is the minimum price which he must at least attain, if he is not, according to his view, to "work" at a loss.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>According to the capitalist view, profit belongs to the costs of production which determine the value of a product. But this "value" now turns out to be nothing other than the production price of the Marxian theory, which in turn can itself only be grasped through the law of value.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Use value, market price, production price – these are the categories which the theories of value that deviate from the labour theory of value set before us as "value." They are either categories which, like use value, have to do with exchange value only insofar as they form its presupposition, but not one of its <em>determining grounds;</em> or such as arise out of exchange value, like production price or market price, which therefore do not explain the exchange relation, but presuppose its explanation for their own explanation.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>These theories content themselves with regarding the notions which buyers and sellers, or the capitalists, have of their business operations as the real grounds of these operations. These theorists believe that they explain a phenomenon scientifically when they compile and reproduce the thoughts of the practitioners about it. But for that no science is needed. Science is supposed to reveal the deeper grounds of social processes and relations, which frequently do not enter the consciousness of those involved at all, or only imperfectly, and often in an entirely distorted form.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>Of the value theories mentioned here, the one that comes closest to the truth is that which seeks the determining ground of value in the costs of production. But it founders on the average profit. Apart from the labour theory of value, none can explain by what the <em>magnitude</em> of the average profit is determined, why under given conditions it amounts, say, to 10 per cent and not to 100 or 1000. The other theories content themselves with either justifying or psychologically explaining the <em>appropriation</em> of profit. But the profoundest philosophy of law and the finest psychology cannot explain whence profit comes, how it is created.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>For the understanding of social interconnections the theory of profit is of the highest importance. Nevertheless we shall not pursue it further here, but return to the theory of surplus value. The theory of profit is the theory of the distribution of the booty – of surplus value – among the various strata of the ruling classes. The industrial or agricultural capitalist does indeed have surplus value produced, but he cannot keep it all. Not only must he, if he employs his capital in a branch of production in which it has to be of lower organic composition, cede a part of the surplus value to other capitalists who have invested their capitals in branches of production with a higher organic composition: this process of equalisation he does not notice, and it therefore causes him no grief. But he must also – and this he notices very clearly – pay a part of his gain to the money-capitalist from whom he borrows money, as interest on capital; leave a part to the merchant as commercial profit; and finally, if he is an agriculturalist, cede a part as ground-rent either, as a tenant, to the landowner, or – if he is himself a landowner – expend it in paying interest on the capital he had to employ for the purchase of his landed property.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But however important all these relations may be, what interests us here above all is the relation between the capitalist and the worker, and indeed not that between the individual capitalist and the individual worker, but that between the capitalist class and the working class. For this relation, however, the theory of profit does not come into consideration; it is rather apt to obscure it, because it makes the magnitude of profit dependent on a number of circumstances that have nothing whatever to do with the relation between capital and labour.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>But however the profit of the individual capitalist may take shape, in the last analysis its magnitude depends on the magnitude of the surplus value, and hence on the magnitude of the exploitation of the wage-labourers. Above all this holds for the totality of the capitalists, since the totality of profit is equivalent to the totality of surplus value.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>It is not from the laws of profit, but from those of surplus value, that we learn to grasp the class antagonism and the class struggle between capital and labour, and it is likewise from these that we best learn to understand the peculiar character of the capitalist mode of production.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In what follows, therefore, we shall again deal only with value and surplus value, proceeding from the presupposition that the price is equal to the value and the profit equal to the surplus value. Here we must leave out of account the average rate of profit and the prices of production, just as, in calculating the laws of falling bodies, one leaves out of account the resistance of the air.</p>
```

```{=html}
<p>In application to practice, of course, the factors here left out of account must be taken into consideration.</p>
```


---

[↑ Previous: Chapter 3. The Degree of Exploitation of Labour-Power](ab2-kap03.qmd) [↑ Return to Table of Contents](../index.qmd) [Next: Chapter 5. The Working Day ↓](ab2-kap05.qmd)
